identityPage.whoAmIDesc:
Curious by nature, I started by studying life sciences with a degree focused on microbiology, because understanding how systems work has always fascinated me. But another passion took over: video games. From great classics to more niche titles like Warframe, whose game design deeply impacted me, I eventually discovered that this could be a career.

While training in game design, I found something familiar: a huge part of good design relies on understanding human behavior, what we call UX, which goes hand in hand with UI to make an interface pleasant and make people want to return. It's no coincidence that I'm increasingly interested in it. It's a field that extends beyond video games and reaches a much broader audience.

I thus have two complementary facets: on one hand, the desire to design and prototype game mechanics directly in engines, testing an idea, feeling it work. On the other hand, an attraction to a broader field, where the same design principles apply to experiences that impact even more users.
identityPage.diplomaSchool: ICAN - Institut de Création et d'Animation Numérique
identityPage.figmaDesc: Doing screen pages, interactive prototypes, and Art Direction.
identityPage.illustratorDesc: Using vector tools and doing vectorization work.
identityPage.photoshopDesc: Basic tool usage, deleting backgrounds, image retouching, and gradient color adjustments.
identityPage.unityDesc: Making games and prototyping with C# oriented GameObjects.
identityPage.gitDesc: Understanding how it works, using it effectively, and setting it up easily.

intro.subtitle: Technical Game Designer | UX/UI Designer
intro.headline: I design
intro.headlineAccent: worlds and experiences that engage, guide, and leave a lasting impression.
intro.description: As a Technical Game Designer and UX/UI Designer, I bridge the gap between creative vision and robust implementation. I love creating anything that can be remembered by people and leave a mark on their minds.
intro.description2: From intricate gameplay systems to seamless user interfaces, I craft experiences that feel alive, intuitive, and engaging. I believe the best design is the one you feel, not just the one you see.

projects.nextProjectDescription: A new project is coming soon.

contact.label: Let's connect
contact.text: I am currently looking for new opportunities. Whether you have a question or just want to say hi, I'll try my best to get back to you!

gwido.description: Explore Athara 3 with Gwido and his vehicle. Learn the rules of this world, the possibilities of each of your abilities in order to explore this planet, avoid its dangers and discover all its secrets!
gwido.context: A complete student project on Unity 3D, blending exploration and adventure in an immersive universe. The team designed from scratch a third-person exploration game with vehicle mechanics, resource management and narrative progression.
gwido.card1_sol1: Distance compensation based on viewing angle
gwido.card2_char2: Dynamic vision adapted to stealth gameplay
gwido.card2_prob1: 2 different attacks with limited time available
gwido.card2_prob2: Patrols needing to be varied and interesting
gwido.card2_sol2: Modular patrol tool in the inspector
gwido.sec01_desc: A dynamic camera system designed to provide optimal visibility in all situations, from open exploration to stealth infiltration.
gwido.split01_desc: In Gwido, we wanted a smooth camera in 3/4 perspective view. But this choice significantly reduced player visibility — they couldn't see far enough at the bottom of the screen. I combined two mechanisms to restore visibility without breaking the camera feel:
gwido.split01_item1: A slight camera advance in the player's direction to anticipate movement
gwido.split01_item2: A compensation effect based on orientation angle to balance the vision cone
gwido.split02_desc: During infiltration phases, when the player draws their weapon, they regain directional control of the camera. They can then look in any direction without moving — an essential mechanic for observing the space before acting.
gwido.text01_text: To enrich environmental storytelling, I designed three types of scripted cameras to place directly in the levels. These cameras create specific situations when the player enters certain zones.
gwido.text01_item1: Camera that locks onto a specific point in space
gwido.text01_item2: Camera that dynamically looks between the player and a point
gwido.text01_item3: Camera that rotates to a different angle
gwido.text01_note: These cameras can merge their triggers to create more complex behaviors. All parameters — distance, angle, transitions — are configurable directly in the Unity inspector.
gwido.grid04_cap1: Between — look between player and point
gwido.grid04_cap2: Target — locks onto a specific point
gwido.carousel02_label: Demos — the 4 scripted behaviors in action
gwido.sec02_desc: An intelligent and aggressive enemy, designed to integrate with stealth and combat gameplay while offering variety despite time constraints.
gwido.split03_title: A Complete FSM for a Living Enemy
gwido.split03_desc: I built a Finite State Machine (FSM) to manage all enemy states. The FSM structures behavior into 4 main states centered around the dynamic vision system.
gwido.split03_item1: Idle — the enemy is at rest, unalerted
gwido.split03_item2: Wander — moving within its patrol zone
gwido.split03_item3: Search — actively searching after losing the player
gwido.split03_item4: Chase — pursuit and attack (2 different modes)
gwido.split04_title: A Rage System Adapted to Stealth
gwido.split04_desc: For the FSM to enable credible stealth gameplay, I developed a dynamic vision system based on a progressive "rage" mechanism: the closer the player is to the enemy, the more reactive it becomes. This progressiveness creates natural tension that rewards discretion and punishes recklessness.
gwido.text02_text: To create a varied enemy within the available time, I designed a fully configurable modular attack system in the Unity inspector. Rather than two radically different attacks, I opted for the same mechanical base differentiated by its metrics:
gwido.text02_item1: Basic attack — short range, fast chaining, melee
gwido.text02_item2: Special attack — long range, long distance traveled, slower chaining
gwido.split05_title: A Modular Tool for Level Designers
gwido.split05_desc: For enemies to integrate naturally into level design without requiring additional code, I developed a custom patrol tool directly in the Unity inspector. It allows composing complex and varied routes, adapted to each zone of the level.
gwido.split05_note: Waypoints, speed, wait time at each waypoint and special behaviors are all configurable in a few clicks.
gwido.grid08_cap1: Enemy on patrol — player detection during passage

eom.description: Live an ephemeral musical experience by diving into your memories.
eom.context: Echoes Of Memories is a first-person experience where the player explores an abstract and minimalist universe. Its only mechanic: summoning a black hole capable of attracting then repelling floating fragments. Each interaction generates unique sounds, transforming the environment into a living musical score.
eom.card1_prob1: Creating music without the player's musical knowledge
eom.card1_prob2: Guaranteeing sonic harmony regardless of action
eom.card1_sol1: Sounds generated based on projection speed and angle
eom.card1_sol2: Predefined musical scale to always sound right
eom.card2_prob1: Providing an expressive tool without complex interface
eom.card2_prob2: Making control intuitive for all players
eom.card2_sol1: Simple two-state: attraction / repulsion on click
eom.sec01_desc: The project's goal was not to create a game with rules, but a free toy — a space where every interaction becomes a note and the player composes without knowing it.
eom.split01_desc: The project's constraint was strong: don't create a game, but a free toy for the player. We designed a musical sandbox where each cluster of fragments produces a dynamic sound based on how it's projected — its speed, angle, and interaction with other clusters.
eom.split01_item1: Each floating fragment is a potential virtual instrument
eom.split01_item2: The generated sound depends on the physics of the interaction
eom.split01_item3: No objective, no rules: just exploration
eom.sec02_desc: To give the player total creative control over their musical composition, I implemented a Time Stop and Rewind system allowing position correction of clusters and music recreation with precision.
eom.split02_desc: The Time Stop and Rewind implementation allows freezing time to correct cluster positions, then rewinding to recreate the musical sequence with exactly the desired tone.
eom.split02_item1: Time Stop: freezes all clusters mid-air for repositioning
eom.split02_item2: Rewind: goes back in the sequence to redo a musical moment
eom.split02_item3: Maximum creative precision without interface complexity
eom.split02_note: This system transforms a mistake into an opportunity — the player can always correct and reinvent their score.
eom.sec03_desc: The only game mechanic: a personal black hole the player can activate to attract or repel floating fragments. Simple in principle, rich in expressive possibilities.
eom.split03_desc: The black hole works on two distinct states: attraction and repulsion. By alternating between the two, the player orchestrates fragment movement in space — each projection, each collision generates a unique sound that integrates into the overall composition.
eom.split03_item1: State 1 — Attraction: fragments are pulled toward the black hole
eom.split03_item2: State 2 — Repulsion: fragments are projected in all directions
eom.split03_item3: The combination of both creates unique sonic patterns
eom.sec04_desc: A complete gameplay session illustrating free exploration, the black hole mechanic and dynamic music generation.
